 344: def check_meta(x, meta, funcname=None, numeric_equal=True):
 345:     """Check that the dask metadata matches the result.
 346: 
 347:     If metadata matches, ``x`` is passed through unchanged. A nice error is
 348:     raised if metadata doesn't match.
 349: 
 350:     Parameters
 351:     ----------
 352:     x : DataFrame, Series, or Index
 353:     meta : DataFrame, Series, or Index
 354:         The expected metadata that ``x`` should match
 355:     funcname : str, optional
 356:         The name of the function in which the metadata was specified. If
 357:         provided, the function name will be included in the error message to be
 358:         more helpful to users.
 359:     numeric_equal : bool, optionl
 360:         If True, integer and floating dtypes compare equal. This is useful due
 361:         to panda's implicit conversion of integer to floating upon encountering
 362:         missingness, which is hard to infer statically.
 363:     """
 364:     eq_types = {"i", "f", "u"} if numeric_equal else set()
 365: 
 366:     def equal_dtypes(a, b):
 367:         if is_categorical_dtype(a) != is_categorical_dtype(b):
 368:             return False
 369:         if isinstance(a, str) and a == "-" or isinstance(b, str) and b == "-":
 370:             return False
 371:         if is_categorical_dtype(a) and is_categorical_dtype(b):
 372:             if UNKNOWN_CATEGORIES in a.categories or UNKNOWN_CATEGORIES in b.categories:
 373:                 return True
 374:             return a == b
 375:         return (a.kind in eq_types and b.kind in eq_types) or is_dtype_equal(a, b)
 376: 
 377:     if not (
 378:         is_dataframe_like(meta) or is_series_like(meta) or is_index_like(meta)
 379:     ) or is_dask_collection(meta):
 380:         raise TypeError(
 381:             "Expected partition to be DataFrame, Series, or "
 382:             "Index, got `%s`" % typename(type(meta))
 383:         )
 384: 
 385:     # Notice, we use .__class__ as opposed to type() in order to support
 386:     # object proxies see <https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/6981>
 387:     if x.__class__ != meta.__class__:
 388:         errmsg = "Expected partition of type `{}` but got `{}`".format(
 389:             typename(type(meta)),
 390:             typename(type(x)),
 391:         )
 392:     elif is_dataframe_like(meta):
 393:         dtypes = pd.concat([x.dtypes, meta.dtypes], axis=1, sort=True)
 394:         bad_dtypes = [
 395:             (repr(col), a, b)
 396:             for col, a, b in dtypes.fillna("-").itertuples()
 397:             if not equal_dtypes(a, b)
 398:         ]
 399:         if bad_dtypes:
 400:             errmsg = "Partition type: `{}`\n{}".format(
 401:                 typename(type(meta)),
 402:                 asciitable(["Column", "Found", "Expected"], bad_dtypes),
 403:             )
 404:         else:
 405:             check_matching_columns(meta, x)
 406:             return x
 407:     else:
 408:         if equal_dtypes(x.dtype, meta.dtype):
 409:             return x
 410:         errmsg = "Partition type: `{}`\n{}".format(
 411:             typename(type(meta)),
 412:             asciitable(["", "dtype"], [("Found", x.dtype), ("Expected", meta.dtype)]),
 413:         )
 414: 
 415:     raise ValueError(
 416:         "Metadata mismatch found%s.\n\n"
 417:         "%s" % ((" in `%s`" % funcname if funcname else ""), errmsg)
 418:     )
 419: 
 420: 
 421: def check_matching_columns(meta, actual):
 422:     # Need nan_to_num otherwise nan comparison gives False
 423:     if not np.array_equal(np.nan_to_num(meta.columns), np.nan_to_num(actual.columns)):
 424:         extra = methods.tolist(actual.columns.difference(meta.columns))
 425:         missing = methods.tolist(meta.columns.difference(actual.columns))
 426:         if extra or missing:
 427:             extra_info = f"  Extra:   {extra}\n  Missing: {missing}"
 428:         else:
 429:             extra_info = "Order of columns does not match"
 430:         raise ValueError(
 431:             "The columns in the computed data do not match"
 432:             " the columns in the provided metadata\n"
 433:             f"{extra_info}"
 434:         )
 435: 
 436: 
 437: def index_summary(idx, name=None):
 438:     """Summarized representation of an Index."""
 439:     n = len(idx)
 440:     if name is None:
 441:         name = idx.__class__.__name__
 442:     if n:
 443:         head = idx[0]
 444:         tail = idx[-1]
 445:         summary = f", {head} to {tail}"
 446:     else:
 447:         summary = ""
 448: 
 449:     return f"{name}: {n} entries{summary}"
 450:

 457: def _check_dask(dsk, check_names=True, check_dtypes=True, result=None, scheduler=None):
 458:     import dask.dataframe as dd
 459: 
 460:     if hasattr(dsk, "__dask_graph__"):
 461:         graph = dsk.__dask_graph__()
 462:         if hasattr(graph, "validate"):
 463:             graph.validate()
 464:         if result is None:
 465:             result = dsk.compute(scheduler=scheduler)
 466:         if isinstance(dsk, dd.Index) or is_index_like(dsk._meta):
 467:             assert "Index" in type(result).__name__, type(result)
 468:             # assert type(dsk._meta) == type(result), type(dsk._meta)
 469:             if check_names:
 470:                 assert dsk.name == result.name
 471:                 assert dsk._meta.name == result.name
 472:                 if isinstance(result, pd.MultiIndex):
 473:                     assert result.names == dsk._meta.names
 474:             if check_dtypes:
 475:                 assert_dask_dtypes(dsk, result)
 476:         elif isinstance(dsk, dd.Series) or is_series_like(dsk._meta):
 477:             assert "Series" in type(result).__name__, type(result)
 478:             assert type(dsk._meta) == type(result), type(dsk._meta)
 479:             if check_names:
 480:                 assert dsk.name == result.name, (dsk.name, result.name)
 481:                 assert dsk._meta.name == result.name
 482:             if check_dtypes:
 483:                 assert_dask_dtypes(dsk, result)
 484:             _check_dask(
 485:                 dsk.index,
 486:                 check_names=check_names,
 487:                 check_dtypes=check_dtypes,
 488:                 result=result.index,
 489:             )
 490:         elif isinstance(dsk, dd.DataFrame) or is_dataframe_like(dsk._meta):
 491:             assert "DataFrame" in type(result).__name__, type(result)
 492:             assert isinstance(dsk.columns, pd.Index), type(dsk.columns)
 493:             assert type(dsk._meta) == type(result), type(dsk._meta)
 494:             if check_names:
 495:                 tm.assert_index_equal(dsk.columns, result.columns)
 496:                 tm.assert_index_equal(dsk._meta.columns, result.columns)
 497:             if check_dtypes:
 498:                 assert_dask_dtypes(dsk, result)
 499:             _check_dask(
 500:                 dsk.index,
 501:                 check_names=check_names,
 502:                 check_dtypes=check_dtypes,
 503:                 result=result.index,
 504:             )
 505:         elif isinstance(dsk, dd.core.Scalar):
 506:             assert np.isscalar(result) or isinstance(
 507:                 result, (pd.Timestamp, pd.Timedelta)
 508:             )
 509:             if check_dtypes:
 510:                 assert_dask_dtypes(dsk, result)
 511:         else:
 512:             msg = f"Unsupported dask instance {type(dsk)} found"
 513:             raise AssertionError(msg)
 514:         return result
 515:     return dsk

 518: def _maybe_sort(a, check_index: bool):
 519:     # sort by value, then index
 520:     try:
 521:         if is_dataframe_like(a):
 522:             if set(a.index.names) & set(a.columns):
 523:                 a.index.names = [
 524:                     "-overlapped-index-name-%d" % i for i in range(len(a.index.names))
 525:                 ]
 526:             a = a.sort_values(by=methods.tolist(a.columns))
 527:         else:
 528:             a = a.sort_values()
 529:     except (TypeError, IndexError, ValueError):
 530:         pass
 531:     return a.sort_index() if check_index else a

 569:             at = type(np.asarray(a.divisions).tolist()[0])  # numpy to python
 570:             bt = type(np.asarray(b.divisions).tolist()[0])  # scalar conversion
 571:             assert at == bt, (at, bt)

 579:     )
 580:     if hasattr(a, "to_pandas"):

 582:     if hasattr(b, "to_pandas"):

 584: 
 585:     a, b = _maybe_convert_string(a, b)

 587:     if isinstance(a, (pd.DataFrame, pd.Series)) and sort_results:
 588:         a = _maybe_sort(a, check_index)
 589:         b = _maybe_sort(b, check_index)
 590:     if not check_index:
 591:         a = a.reset_index(drop=True)
 592:         b = b.reset_index(drop=True)
 593:     if isinstance(a, pd.DataFrame):

 596:         )
 597:     elif isinstance(a, pd.Series):

 600:         )

 602:         tm.assert_index_equal(a, b, exact=check_dtype, **kwargs)
 603:     else:

 606:         else:
 607:             if np.isnan(a):
 608:                 assert np.isnan(b)
 609:             else:

 626: def assert_divisions(ddf, scheduler=None):
 627:     if not hasattr(ddf, "divisions"):
 628:         return
 629: 
 630:     assert isinstance(ddf.divisions, tuple)
 631: 
 632:     if not getattr(ddf, "known_divisions", False):
 633:         return
 634: 
 635:     def index(x):
 636:         if is_index_like(x):
 637:             return x
 638:         try:
 639:             return x.index.get_level_values(0)
 640:         except AttributeError:
 641:             return x.index
 642: 
 643:     get = get_scheduler(scheduler=scheduler, collections=[type(ddf)])
 644:     results = get(ddf.dask, ddf.__dask_keys__())
 645:     for i, df in enumerate(results[:-1]):
 646:         if len(df):
 647:             assert index(df).min() >= ddf.divisions[i]
 648:             assert index(df).max() < ddf.divisions[i + 1]
 649: 
 650:     if len(results[-1]):
 651:         assert index(results[-1]).min() >= ddf.divisions[-2]

 662:         assert len(k) < 100
 663:         assert " " not in k
 664:         assert k.split("-")[0].isidentifier(), k

 667: def assert_dask_dtypes(ddf, res, numeric_equal=True):
 668:     """Check that the dask metadata matches the result.
 669: 
 670:     If `numeric_equal`, integer and floating dtypes compare equal. This is
 671:     useful due to the implicit conversion of integer to floating upon
 672:     encountering missingness, which is hard to infer statically."""
 673: 
 674:     eq_type_sets = [{"O", "S", "U", "a"}]  # treat object and strings alike
 675:     if numeric_equal:
 676:         eq_type_sets.append({"i", "f", "u"})
 677: 
 678:     def eq_dtypes(a, b):
 679:         return any(
 680:             a.kind in eq_types and b.kind in eq_types for eq_types in eq_type_sets
 681:         ) or (a == b)
 682: 
 683:     if not is_dask_collection(res) and is_dataframe_like(res):
 684:         for a, b in pd.concat([ddf._meta.dtypes, res.dtypes], axis=1).itertuples(
 685:             index=False
 686:         ):
 687:             assert eq_dtypes(a, b)
 688:     elif not is_dask_collection(res) and (is_index_like(res) or is_series_like(res)):
 689:         a = ddf._meta.dtype
 690:         b = res.dtype
 691:         assert eq_dtypes(a, b)
 692:     else:
 693:         if hasattr(ddf._meta, "dtype"):
 694:             a = ddf._meta.dtype
 695:             if not hasattr(res, "dtype"):
 696:                 assert np.isscalar(res)
 697:                 b = np.dtype(type(res))
 698:             else:
 699:                 b = res.dtype
 700:             assert eq_dtypes(a, b)
 701:         else:
 702:             assert type(ddf._meta) == type(res)

 709:     else:

 713: def valid_divisions(divisions):
 714:     """Are the provided divisions valid?
 715: 
 716:     Examples
 717:     --------
 718:     >>> valid_divisions([1, 2, 3])
 719:     True
 720:     >>> valid_divisions([3, 2, 1])
 721:     False
 722:     >>> valid_divisions([1, 1, 1])
 723:     False
 724:     >>> valid_divisions([0, 1, 1])
 725:     True
 726:     >>> valid_divisions(123)
 727:     False
 728:     >>> valid_divisions([0, float('nan'), 1])
 729:     False
 730:     """
 731:     if not isinstance(divisions, (tuple, list)):
 732:         return False
 733: 
 734:     if pd.isnull(divisions).any():
 735:         return False
 736: 
 737:     for i, x in enumerate(divisions[:-2]):
 738:         if x >= divisions[i + 1]:
 739:             return False
 740:         if isinstance(x, Number) and math.isnan(x):
 741:             return False
 742: 
 743:     for x in divisions[-2:]:
 744:         if isinstance(x, Number) and math.isnan(x):
 745:             return False
 746: 
 747:     if divisions[-2] > divisions[-1]:
 748:         return False
 749: 
 750:     return True

 753: def drop_by_shallow_copy(df, columns, errors="raise"):
 754:     """Use shallow copy to drop columns in place"""
 755:     df2 = df.copy(deep=False)
 756:     if not pd.api.types.is_list_like(columns):
 757:         columns = [columns]
 758:     df2.drop(columns=columns, inplace=True, errors=errors)
 759:     return df2

 767:     """Return a serial DataFrame constructor
 768: 
 769:     Parameters
 770:     ----------
 771:     like :
 772:         Any series-like, Index-like or dataframe-like object.
 773:     """
 774:     if is_dask_collection(like):
 775:         try:
 776:             like = like._meta
 777:         except AttributeError:
 778:             raise TypeError(f"{type(like)} not supported by meta_frame_constructor")
 779:     if is_dataframe_like(like):
 780:         return like._constructor
 781:     elif is_series_like(like):
 782:         return like._constructor_expanddim
 783:     elif is_index_like(like):

  43: def _register_command_ep(interface, entry_point):
  44:     """Add `entry_point` command to `interface`.
  45: 
  46:     Parameters
  47:     ----------
  48:     interface : click.Command or click.Group
  49:         The click interface to augment with `entry_point`.
  50:     entry_point : importlib.metadata.EntryPoint
  51:         The entry point which loads to a ``click.Command`` or
  52:         ``click.Group`` instance to be added as a sub-command or
  53:         sub-group in `interface`.
  54: 
  55:     """
  56:     command = entry_point.load()
  57:     if not isinstance(command, (click.Command, click.Group)):
  58:         warnings.warn(
  59:             "entry points in 'dask_cli' must be instances of "
  60:             f"click.Command or click.Group, not {type(command)}."
  61:         )
  62:         return
  63: 
  64:     if command.name in interface.commands:
  65:         warnings.warn(
  66:             f"While registering the command with name '{command.name}', an "
  67:             "existing command or group; the original has been overwritten."
  68:         )
  69: 
  70:     interface.add_command(command)
  71: 
  72: 
  73: def run_cli():
  74:     """Run the dask command line interface."""
  75: 
  76:     # discover "dask_cli" entry points and try to register them to the
  77:     # top level `cli`.
  78:     for ep in importlib_metadata.entry_points(group="dask_cli"):
  79:         _register_command_ep(cli, ep)

  59: def canonical_name(k: str, config: dict) -> str:
  60:     """Return the canonical name for a key.
  61: 
  62:     Handles user choice of '-' or '_' conventions by standardizing on whichever
  63:     version was set first. If a key already exists in either hyphen or
  64:     underscore form, the existing version is the canonical name. If neither
  65:     version exists the original key is used as is.
  66:     """
  67:     try:
  68:         if k in config:
  69:             return k
  70:     except TypeError:
  71:         # config is not a mapping, return the same name as provided
  72:         return k
  73: 
  74:     altk = k.replace("_", "-") if "_" in k else k.replace("-", "_")
  75: 
  76:     if altk in config:
  77:         return altk
  78: 
  79:     return k

  82: def update(old: dict, new: Mapping, priority: Literal["old", "new"] = "new") -> dict:
  83:     """Update a nested dictionary with values from another
  84: 
  85:     This is like dict.update except that it smoothly merges nested values
  86: 
  87:     This operates in-place and modifies old
  88: 
  89:     Parameters
  90:     ----------
  91:     priority: string {'old', 'new'}
  92:         If new (default) then the new dictionary has preference.
  93:         Otherwise the old dictionary does.
  94: 
  95:     Examples
  96:     --------
  97:     >>> a = {'x': 1, 'y': {'a': 2}}
  98:     >>> b = {'x': 2, 'y': {'b': 3}}
  99:     >>> update(a, b)  # doctest: +SKIP
 100:     {'x': 2, 'y': {'a': 2, 'b': 3}}
 101: 
 102:     >>> a = {'x': 1, 'y': {'a': 2}}
 103:     >>> b = {'x': 2, 'y': {'b': 3}}
 104:     >>> update(a, b, priority='old')  # doctest: +SKIP
 105:     {'x': 1, 'y': {'a': 2, 'b': 3}}
 106: 
 107:     See Also
 108:     --------
 109:     dask.config.merge
 110:     """
 111:     for k, v in new.items():
 112:         k = canonical_name(k, old)
 113: 
 114:         if isinstance(v, Mapping):
 115:             if k not in old or old[k] is None:
 116:                 old[k] = {}
 117:             update(old[k], v, priority=priority)
 118:         else:
 119:             if priority == "new" or k not in old:
 120:                 old[k] = v
 121: 
 122:     return old
 123:

 125: def merge(*dicts: Mapping) -> dict:
 126:     """Update a sequence of nested dictionaries
 127: 
 128:     This prefers the values in the latter dictionaries to those in the former
 129: 
 130:     Examples
 131:     --------
 132:     >>> a = {'x': 1, 'y': {'a': 2}}
 133:     >>> b = {'y': {'b': 3}}
 134:     >>> merge(a, b)  # doctest: +SKIP
 135:     {'x': 1, 'y': {'a': 2, 'b': 3}}
 136: 
 137:     See Also
 138:     --------
 139:     dask.config.update
 140:     """
 141:     result: dict = {}
 142:     for d in dicts:
 143:         update(result, d)
 144:     return result

 147: def _load_config_file(path: str) -> dict | None:
 148:     """A helper for loading a config file from a path, and erroring
 149:     appropriately if the file is malformed."""
 150:     try:
 151:         with open(path) as f:
 152:             config = yaml.safe_load(f.read())
 153:     except OSError:
 154:         # Ignore permission errors
 155:         return None
 156:     except Exception as exc:
 157:         raise ValueError(
 158:             f"A dask config file at {path!r} is malformed, original error "
 159:             f"message:\n\n{exc}"
 160:         ) from None
 161:     if config is not None and not isinstance(config, dict):
 162:         raise ValueError(
 163:             f"A dask config file at {path!r} is malformed - config files must have "
 164:             f"a dict as the top level object, got a {type(config).__name__} instead"
 165:         )
 166:     return config
 167: 
 168: 
 169: def collect_yaml(paths: Sequence[str] = paths) -> list[dict]:
 170:     """Collect configuration from yaml files
 171: 
 172:     This searches through a list of paths, expands to find all yaml or json
 173:     files, and then parses each file.
 174:     """
 175:     # Find all paths
 176:     file_paths = []
 177:     for path in paths:
 178:         if os.path.exists(path):
 179:             if os.path.isdir(path):
 180:                 try:
 181:                     file_paths.extend(
 182:                         sorted(
 183:                             os.path.join(path, p)
 184:                             for p in os.listdir(path)
 185:                             if os.path.splitext(p)[1].lower()
 186:                             in (".json", ".yaml", ".yml")
 187:                         )
 188:                     )
 189:                 except OSError:
 190:                     # Ignore permission errors
 191:                     pass
 192:             else:
 193:                 file_paths.append(path)
 194: 
 195:     configs = []
 196: 
 197:     # Parse yaml files
 198:     for path in file_paths:
 199:         config = _load_config_file(path)
 200:         if config is not None:
 201:             configs.append(config)
 202: 
 203:     return configs
 204: 
 205: 
 206: def collect_env(env: Mapping[str, str] | None = None) -> dict:
 207:     """Collect config from environment variables
 208: 
 209:     This grabs environment variables of the form "DASK_FOO__BAR_BAZ=123" and
 210:     turns these into config variables of the form ``{"foo": {"bar-baz": 123}}``
 211:     It transforms the key and value in the following way:
 212: 
 213:     -  Lower-cases the key text
 214:     -  Treats ``__`` (double-underscore) as nested access
 215:     -  Calls ``ast.literal_eval`` on the value
 216: 
 217:     Any serialized config passed via ``DASK_INTERNAL_INHERIT_CONFIG`` is also set here.
 218: 
 219:     """
 220: 
 221:     if env is None:
 222:         env = os.environ
 223: 
 224:     if "DASK_INTERNAL_INHERIT_CONFIG" in env:
 225:         d = deserialize(env["DASK_INTERNAL_INHERIT_CONFIG"])
 226:     else:
 227:         d = {}
 228: 
 229:     for name, value in env.items():
 230:         if name.startswith("DASK_"):
 231:             varname = name[5:].lower().replace("__", ".")
 232:             try:
 233:                 d[varname] = ast.literal_eval(value)
 234:             except (SyntaxError, ValueError):
 235:                 d[varname] = value
 236: 
 237:     result: dict = {}
 238:     set(d, config=result)
 239:     return result
 240:

 242: def ensure_file(
 243:     source: str, destination: str | None = None, comment: bool = True
 244: ) -> None:
 245:     """
 246:     Copy file to default location if it does not already exist
 247: 
 248:     This tries to move a default configuration file to a default location if
 249:     if does not already exist.  It also comments out that file by default.
 250: 
 251:     This is to be used by downstream modules (like dask.distributed) that may
 252:     have default configuration files that they wish to include in the default
 253:     configuration path.
 254: 
 255:     Parameters
 256:     ----------
 257:     source : string, filename
 258:         Source configuration file, typically within a source directory.
 259:     destination : string, directory
 260:         Destination directory. Configurable by ``DASK_CONFIG`` environment
 261:         variable, falling back to ~/.config/dask.
 262:     comment : bool, True by default
 263:         Whether or not to comment out the config file when copying.
 264:     """
 265:     if destination is None:
 266:         destination = PATH
 267: 
 268:     # destination is a file and already exists, never overwrite
 269:     if os.path.isfile(destination):
 270:         return
 271: 
 272:     # If destination is not an existing file, interpret as a directory,
 273:     # use the source basename as the filename
 274:     directory = destination
 275:     destination = os.path.join(directory, os.path.basename(source))
 276: 
 277:     try:
 278:         if not os.path.exists(destination):
 279:             os.makedirs(directory, exist_ok=True)
 280: 
 281:             # Atomically create destination.  Parallel testing discovered
 282:             # a race condition where a process can be busy creating the
 283:             # destination while another process reads an empty config file.
 284:             tmp = "%s.tmp.%d" % (destination, os.getpid())
 285:             with open(source) as f:
 286:                 lines = list(f)
 287: 
 288:             if comment:
 289:                 lines = [
 290:                     "# " + line if line.strip() and not line.startswith("#") else line
 291:                     for line in lines
 292:                 ]
 293: 
 294:             with open(tmp, "w") as f:
 295:                 f.write("".join(lines))
 296: 
 297:             try:
 298:                 os.rename(tmp, destination)
 299:             except OSError:
 300:                 os.remove(tmp)
 301:     except OSError:
 302:         pass

 305: class set:
 306:     """Temporarily set configuration values within a context manager
 307: 
 308:     Parameters
 309:     ----------
 310:     arg : mapping or None, optional
 311:         A mapping of configuration key-value pairs to set.
 312:     **kwargs :
 313:         Additional key-value pairs to set. If ``arg`` is provided, values set
 314:         in ``arg`` will be applied before those in ``kwargs``.
 315:         Double-underscores (``__``) in keyword arguments will be replaced with
 316:         ``.``, allowing nested values to be easily set.
 317: 
 318:     Examples
 319:     --------
 320:     >>> import dask
 321: 
 322:     Set ``'foo.bar'`` in a context, by providing a mapping.
 323: 
 324:     >>> with dask.config.set({'foo.bar': 123}):
 325:     ...     pass
 326: 
 327:     Set ``'foo.bar'`` in a context, by providing a keyword argument.
 328: 
 329:     >>> with dask.config.set(foo__bar=123):
 330:     ...     pass
 331: 
 332:     Set ``'foo.bar'`` globally.
 333: 
 334:     >>> dask.config.set(foo__bar=123)  # doctest: +SKIP
 335: 
 336:     See Also
 337:     --------
 338:     dask.config.get
 339:     """
 340: 
 341:     config: dict
 342:     # [(op, path, value), ...]
 343:     _record: list[tuple[Literal["insert", "replace"], tuple[str, ...], Any]]
 344: 
 345:     def __init__(
 346:         self,
 347:         arg: Mapping | None = None,
 348:         config: dict = config,
 349:         lock: threading.Lock = config_lock,
 350:         **kwargs,
 351:     ):
 352:         with lock:
 353:             self.config = config
 354:             self._record = []
 355: 
 356:             if arg is not None:
 357:                 for key, value in arg.items():
 358:                     key = check_deprecations(key)
 359:                     self._assign(key.split("."), value, config)
 360:             if kwargs:
 361:                 for key, value in kwargs.items():
 362:                     key = key.replace("__", ".")
 363:                     key = check_deprecations(key)
 364:                     self._assign(key.split("."), value, config)
 365: 
 366:     def __enter__(self):
 367:         return self.config
 368: 
 369:     def __exit__(self, type, value, traceback):
 370:         for op, path, value in reversed(self._record):
 371:             d = self.config
 372:             if op == "replace":
 373:                 for key in path[:-1]:
 374:                     d = d.setdefault(key, {})
 375:                 d[path[-1]] = value
 376:             else:  # insert
 377:                 for key in path[:-1]:
 378:                     try:
 379:                         d = d[key]
 380:                     except KeyError:
 381:                         break
 382:                 else:
 383:                     d.pop(path[-1], None)
 384: 
 385:     def _assign(
 386:         self,
 387:         keys: Sequence[str],
 388:         value: Any,
 389:         d: dict,
 390:         path: tuple[str, ...] = (),
 391:         record: bool = True,
 392:     ) -> None:
 393:         """Assign value into a nested configuration dictionary
 394: 
 395:         Parameters
 396:         ----------
 397:         keys : Sequence[str]
 398:             The nested path of keys to assign the value.
 399:         value : object
 400:         d : dict
 401:             The part of the nested dictionary into which we want to assign the
 402:             value
 403:         path : tuple[str], optional
 404:             The path history up to this point.
 405:         record : bool, optional
 406:             Whether this operation needs to be recorded to allow for rollback.
 407:         """
 408:         key = canonical_name(keys[0], d)
 409: 
 410:         path = path + (key,)
 411: 
 412:         if len(keys) == 1:
 413:             if record:
 414:                 if key in d:
 415:                     self._record.append(("replace", path, d[key]))
 416:                 else:
 417:                     self._record.append(("insert", path, None))
 418:             d[key] = value
 419:         else:
 420:             if key not in d:
 421:                 if record:
 422:                     self._record.append(("insert", path, None))
 423:                 d[key] = {}
 424:                 # No need to record subsequent operations after an insert
 425:                 record = False
 426:             self._assign(keys[1:], value, d[key], path, record=record)
 427: 
 428: 
 429: def collect(paths: list[str] = paths, env: Mapping[str, str] | None = None) -> dict:
 430:     """
 431:     Collect configuration from paths and environment variables
 432: 
 433:     Parameters
 434:     ----------
 435:     paths : list[str]
 436:         A list of paths to search for yaml config files
 437: 
 438:     env : Mapping[str, str]
 439:         The system environment variables
 440: 
 441:     Returns
 442:     -------
 443:     config: dict
 444: 
 445:     See Also
 446:     --------
 447:     dask.config.refresh: collect configuration and update into primary config
 448:     """
 449:     if env is None:
 450:         env = os.environ
 451: 
 452:     configs = collect_yaml(paths=paths)
 453:     configs.append(collect_env(env=env))
 454: 
 455:     return merge(*configs)
 456:

 458: def refresh(
 459:     config: dict = config, defaults: list[Mapping] = defaults, **kwargs
 460: ) -> None:
 461:     """
 462:     Update configuration by re-reading yaml files and env variables
 463: 
 464:     This mutates the global dask.config.config, or the config parameter if
 465:     passed in.
 466: 
 467:     This goes through the following stages:
 468: 
 469:     1.  Clearing out all old configuration
 470:     2.  Updating from the stored defaults from downstream libraries
 471:         (see update_defaults)
 472:     3.  Updating from yaml files and environment variables
 473: 
 474:     Note that some functionality only checks configuration once at startup and
 475:     may not change behavior, even if configuration changes.  It is recommended
 476:     to restart your python process if convenient to ensure that new
 477:     configuration changes take place.
 478: 
 479:     See Also
 480:     --------
 481:     dask.config.collect: for parameters
 482:     dask.config.update_defaults
 483:     """
 484:     config.clear()
 485: 
 486:     for d in defaults:
 487:         update(config, d, priority="old")
 488: 
 489:     update(config, collect(**kwargs))
 490: 
 491: 
 492: def get(
 493:     key: str,
 494:     default: Any = no_default,
 495:     config: dict = config,
 496:     override_with: Any | None = None,
 497: ) -> Any:
 498:     """
 499:     Get elements from global config
 500: 
 501:     If ``override_with`` is not None this value will be passed straight back.
 502:     Useful for getting kwarg defaults from Dask config.
 503: 
 504:     Use '.' for nested access
 505: 
 506:     Examples
 507:     --------
 508:     >>> from dask import config
 509:     >>> config.get('foo')  # doctest: +SKIP
 510:     {'x': 1, 'y': 2}
 511: 
 512:     >>> config.get('foo.x')  # doctest: +SKIP
 513:     1
 514: 
 515:     >>> config.get('foo.x.y', default=123)  # doctest: +SKIP
 516:     123
 517: 
 518:     >>> config.get('foo.y', override_with=None)  # doctest: +SKIP
 519:     2
 520: 
 521:     >>> config.get('foo.y', override_with=3)  # doctest: +SKIP
 522:     3
 523: 
 524:     See Also
 525:     --------
 526:     dask.config.set
 527:     """
 528:     if override_with is not None:
 529:         return override_with
 530:     keys = key.split(".")
 531:     result = config
 532:     for k in keys:
 533:         k = canonical_name(k, result)
 534:         try:
 535:             result = result[k]
 536:         except (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError):
 537:             if default is not no_default:
 538:                 return default
 539:             else:
 540:                 raise
 541:     return result
 542:

 544: def rename(aliases: Mapping, config: dict = config) -> None:
 545:     """Rename old keys to new keys
 546: 
 547:     This helps migrate older configuration versions over time
 548:     """
 549:     old = []
 550:     new = {}
 551:     for o, n in aliases.items():
 552:         value = get(o, None, config=config)
 553:         if value is not None:
 554:             old.append(o)
 555:             new[n] = value
 556: 
 557:     for k in old:
 558:         del config[canonical_name(k, config)]  # TODO: support nested keys
 559: 
 560:     set(new, config=config)
 561: 
 562: 
 563: def update_defaults(
 564:     new: Mapping, config: dict = config, defaults: list[Mapping] = defaults
 565: ) -> None:
 566:     """Add a new set of defaults to the configuration
 567: 
 568:     It does two things:
 569: 
 570:     1.  Add the defaults to a global collection to be used by refresh later
 571:     2.  Updates the global config with the new configuration
 572:         prioritizing older values over newer ones
 573:     """
 574:     defaults.append(new)
 575:     update(config, new, priority="old")
 576: 
 577: 
 578: def expand_environment_variables(config: Any) -> Any:
 579:     """Expand environment variables in a nested config dictionary
 580: 
 581:     This function will recursively search through any nested dictionaries
 582:     and/or lists.
 583: 
 584:     Parameters
 585:     ----------
 586:     config : dict, iterable, or str
 587:         Input object to search for environment variables
 588: 
 589:     Returns
 590:     -------
 591:     config : same type as input
 592: 
 593:     Examples
 594:     --------
 595:     >>> expand_environment_variables({'x': [1, 2, '$USER']})  # doctest: +SKIP
 596:     {'x': [1, 2, 'my-username']}
 597:     """
 598:     if isinstance(config, Mapping):
 599:         return {k: expand_environment_variables(v) for k, v in config.items()}
 600:     elif isinstance(config, str):
 601:         return os.path.expandvars(config)
 602:     elif isinstance(config, (list, tuple, builtins.set)):
 603:         return type(config)(expand_environment_variables(v) for v in config)
 604:     else:
 605:         return config

 630: def check_deprecations(key: str, deprecations: dict = deprecations) -> str:
 631:     """Check if the provided value has been renamed or removed
 632: 
 633:     Parameters
 634:     ----------
 635:     key : str
 636:         The configuration key to check
 637:     deprecations : Dict[str, str]
 638:         The mapping of aliases
 639: 
 640:     Examples
 641:     --------
 642:     >>> deprecations = {"old_key": "new_key", "invalid": None}
 643:     >>> check_deprecations("old_key", deprecations=deprecations)  # doctest: +SKIP
 644:     UserWarning: Configuration key "old_key" has been deprecated. Please use "new_key" instead.
 645: 
 646:     >>> check_deprecations("invalid", deprecations=deprecations)
 647:     Traceback (most recent call last):
 648:         ...
 649:     ValueError: Configuration value "invalid" has been removed
 650: 
 651:     >>> check_deprecations("another_key", deprecations=deprecations)
 652:     'another_key'
 653: 
 654:     Returns
 655:     -------
 656:     new: str
 657:         The proper key, whether the original (if no deprecation) or the aliased
 658:         value
 659:     """
 660:     if key in deprecations:
 661:         new = deprecations[key]
 662:         if new:
 663:             warnings.warn(
 664:                 'Configuration key "{}" has been deprecated. '
 665:                 'Please use "{}" instead'.format(key, new)
 666:             )
 667:             return new
 668:         else:
 669:             raise ValueError(f'Configuration value "{key}" has been removed')
 670:     else:
 671:         return key

 674: def serialize(data: Any) -> str:
 675:     """Serialize config data into a string.
 676: 
 677:     Typically used to pass config via the ``DASK_INTERNAL_INHERIT_CONFIG`` environment variable.
 678: 
 679:     Parameters
 680:     ----------
 681:     data: json-serializable object
 682:         The data to serialize
 683: 
 684:     Returns
 685:     -------
 686:     serialized_data: str
 687:         The serialized data as a string
 688: 
 689:     """
 690:     return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(json.dumps(data).encode()).decode()
 691: 
 692: 
 693: def deserialize(data: str) -> Any:
 694:     """De-serialize config data into the original object.
 695: 
 696:     Typically when receiving config via the ``DASK_INTERNAL_INHERIT_CONFIG`` environment variable.
 697: 
 698:     Parameters
 699:     ----------
 700:     data: str
 701:         String serialized by :func:`dask.config.serialize`
 702: 
 703:     Returns
 704:     -------
 705:     deserialized_data: obj
 706:         The de-serialized data
 707: 
 708:     """
 709:     return json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(data.encode()).decode())

 123:     """Create a dataframe from a set of JSON files

 125:     This utilises ``pandas.read_json()``, and most parameters are
 126:     passed through - see its docstring.
 127: 
 128:     Differences: orient is 'records' by default, with lines=True; this
 129:     is appropriate for line-delimited "JSON-lines" data, the kind of JSON output
 130:     that is most common in big-data scenarios, and which can be chunked when
 131:     reading (see ``read_json()``). All other options require blocksize=None,
 132:     i.e., one partition per input file.
 133: 
 134:     Parameters
 135:     ----------
 136:     url_path: str, list of str

 138:         find a set of file names.

 140:     encoding, errors:

 142:         to errors in the conversion (see ``str.encode()``).

 145:         False otherwise.
 146:     storage_options: dict

 148:     blocksize: None or int

 150:         file. If int, which can only be used for line-delimited JSON files,
 151:         each partition will be approximately this size in bytes, to the nearest
 152:         newline character.

 162:         default, this will be the pandas JSON reader (``pd.read_json``).
 163:     include_path_column : bool or str, optional
 164:         Include a column with the file path where each row in the dataframe
 165:         originated. If ``True``, a new column is added to the dataframe called
 166:         ``path``. If ``str``, sets new column name. Default is ``False``.
 167:     path_converter : function or None, optional

 169:         paths in the ``path`` column, for instance, to strip a common prefix from
 170:         all the paths.

 172: 
 173:     Returns
 174:     -------
 175:     dask.DataFrame
 176: 
 177:     Examples
 178:     --------
 179:     Load single file
 180: 
 181:     >>> dd.read_json('myfile.1.json')  # doctest: +SKIP
 182: 
 183:     Load multiple files
 184: 
 185:     >>> dd.read_json('myfile.*.json')  # doctest: +SKIP
 186: 
 187:     >>> dd.read_json(['myfile.1.json', 'myfile.2.json'])  # doctest: +SKIP
 188:

 190:     256MB size

 192:     >> dd.read_json('data/file*.csv', blocksize=2**28)

 195:         lines = orient == "records"
 196:     if orient != "records" and lines:

 199:         )

 201:         raise ValueError(

 203:             "input (orient='records', lines=True)."
 204:         )

 210:         path_converter = lambda x: x

 212:     if blocksize:
 213:         b_out = read_bytes(
 214:             url_path,
 215:             b"\n",
 216:             blocksize=blocksize,
 217:             sample=sample,
 218:             compression=compression,
 219:             include_path=include_path_column,
 220:             **storage_options,
 221:         )
 222:         if include_path_column:
 223:             first, chunks, paths = b_out
 224:             first_path = path_converter(paths[0])
 225:             path_dtype = pd.CategoricalDtype(path_converter(p) for p in paths)
 226:             flat_paths = flatten(
 227:                 [path_converter(p)] * len(chunk) for p, chunk in zip(paths, chunks)
 228:             )
 229:         else:
 230:             first, chunks = b_out
 231:             first_path = None
 232:             flat_paths = (None,)
 233:             path_dtype = None
 234: 
 235:         flat_chunks = flatten(chunks)
 236:         if meta is None:
 237:             meta = read_json_chunk(
 238:                 first,
 239:                 encoding,
 240:                 errors,
 241:                 engine,
 242:                 include_path_column,
 243:                 first_path,
 244:                 path_dtype,
 245:                 kwargs,
 246:             )
 247:         meta = make_meta(meta)
 248:         parts = [
 249:             delayed(read_json_chunk)(
 250:                 chunk,
 251:                 encoding,
 252:                 errors,
 253:                 engine,
 254:                 include_path_column,
 255:                 path,
 256:                 path_dtype,
 257:                 kwargs,
 258:                 meta=meta,
 259:             )
 260:             for chunk, path in zip_longest(flat_chunks, flat_paths)
 261:         ]
 262:     else:
 263:         files = open_files(
 264:             url_path,
 265:             "rt",
 266:             encoding=encoding,
 267:             errors=errors,
 268:             compression=compression,
 269:             **storage_options,
 270:         )
 271:         path_dtype = pd.CategoricalDtype(path_converter(f.path) for f in files)
 272:         parts = [
 273:             delayed(read_json_file)(
 274:                 f,
 275:                 orient,
 276:                 lines,
 277:                 engine,
 278:                 include_path_column,
 279:                 path_converter(f.path),
 280:                 path_dtype,
 281:                 kwargs,
 282:             )
 283:             for f in files
 284:         ]
 285: 
 286:     return from_delayed(parts, meta=meta)
 287: 
 288: 
 289: def read_json_chunk(
 290:     chunk, encoding, errors, engine, column_name, path, path_dtype, kwargs, meta=None
 291: ):
 292:     s = io.StringIO(chunk.decode(encoding, errors))
 293:     s.seek(0)
 294:     df = engine(s, orient="records", lines=True, **kwargs)
 295:     if meta is not None and df.empty:
 296:         return meta
 297: 
 298:     if column_name:
 299:         df = add_path_column(df, column_name, path, path_dtype)
 300: 
 301:     return df
 302: 
 303: 
 304: def read_json_file(f, orient, lines, engine, column_name, path, path_dtype, kwargs):
 305:     with f as open_file:
 306:         df = engine(open_file, orient=orient, lines=lines, **kwargs)
 307:     if column_name:
 308:         df = add_path_column(df, column_name, path, path_dtype)
 309:     return df
 310: 
 311: 
 312: def add_path_column(df, column_name, path, dtype):
 313:     if column_name in df.columns:
 314:         raise ValueError(
 315:             f"Files already contain the column name: '{column_name}', so the path "
 316:             "column cannot use this name. Please set `include_path_column` to a "
 317:             "unique name."
 318:         )
 319:     return df.assign(**{column_name: pd.Series([path] * len(df), dtype=dtype)})"}

  52: # #############################################
  53: #
  54: # GroupBy implementation notes
  55: #
  56: # Dask groupby supports reductions, i.e., mean, sum and alike, and apply. The
  57: # former do not shuffle the data and are efficiently implemented as tree
  58: # reductions. The latter is implemented by shuffling the underlying partitions
  59: # such that all items of a group can be found in the same partition.
  60: #
  61: # The argument to ``.groupby`` (``by``), can be a ``str``, ``dd.DataFrame``,
  62: # ``dd.Series``, or a list thereof. In operations on the grouped object, the
  63: # divisions of the the grouped object and the items of ``by`` have to align.
  64: # Currently, there is no support to shuffle the ``by`` values as part of the
  65: # groupby operation. Therefore, the alignment has to be guaranteed by the
  66: # caller.
  67: #
  68: # To operate on matching partitions, most groupby operations exploit the
  69: # corresponding support in ``apply_concat_apply``. Specifically, this function
  70: # operates on matching partitions of frame-like objects passed as varargs.
  71: #
  72: # After the initial chunk step, ``by``` is implicitly passed along to
  73: # subsequent operations as the index of the partitions. Groupby operations on
  74: # the individual partitions can then access ``by`` via the ``levels``
  75: # parameter of the ``groupby`` function. The correct argument is determined by
  76: # the ``_determine_levels`` function.
  77: #
  78: # To minimize overhead, any ``by`` that is a series contained within the
  79: # dataframe is passed as a columnn key. This transformation is implemented as
  80: # ``_normalize_by``.
  81: #
  82: # #############################################

 103: def _determine_levels(by):
 104:     """Determine the correct levels argument to groupby."""
 105:     if isinstance(by, (tuple, list)) and len(by) > 1:
 106:         return list(range(len(by)))

 111: def _determine_shuffle(shuffle, split_out):
 112:     """Determine the default shuffle behavior based on split_out"""
 113:     if shuffle is None:
 114:         if split_out > 1:

 119:             # https://github.com/dask/distributed/issues/5502
 120:             return config.get("dataframe.shuffle.method", None) or "tasks"
 121:         else:

 126: def _normalize_by(df, by):
 127:     """Replace series with column names wherever possible."""
 128:     if not isinstance(df, DataFrame):
 129:         return by

 131:     elif isinstance(by, list):

 161: def _is_aligned(df, by):

 164:         return df.index.equals(by.index)

 171: def _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, **kwargs):
 172:     """Groupby, but raise if df and `by` key are unaligned.
 173: 
 174:     Pandas supports grouping by a column that doesn't align with the input
 175:     frame/series/index. However, the reindexing does not seem to be
 176:     threadsafe, and can result in incorrect results. Since grouping by an
 177:     unaligned key is generally a bad idea, we just error loudly in dask.
 178: 
 179:     For more information see pandas GH issue #15244 and Dask GH issue #1876."""
 180:     by = kwargs.get("by", None)
 181:     if by is not None and not _is_aligned(df, by):
 182:         msg = (
 183:             "Grouping by an unaligned column is unsafe and unsupported.\n"
 184:             "This can be caused by filtering only one of the object or\n"
 185:             "grouping key. For example, the following works in pandas,\n"
 186:             "but not in dask:\n"
 187:             "\n"
 188:             "df[df.foo < 0].groupby(df.bar)\n"
 189:             "\n"
 190:             "This can be avoided by either filtering beforehand, or\n"
 191:             "passing in the name of the column instead:\n"
 192:             "\n"
 193:             "df2 = df[df.foo < 0]\n"
 194:             "df2.groupby(df2.bar)\n"
 195:             "# or\n"
 196:             "df[df.foo < 0].groupby('bar')\n"
 197:             "\n"
 198:             "For more information see dask GH issue #1876."
 199:         )

 370:                             "The default value of numeric_only will be changed to False "
 371:                             "in the future when using dask with pandas 2.0",
 372:                             FutureWarning,
 373:                         )
 374:         return func(self, *args, **kwargs)
 375: 
 376:     return wrapper
 377: 
 378: 
 379: ###############################################################
 380: # Aggregation
 381: ###############################################################
 382: 
 383: 
 384: class Aggregation:
 385:     """User defined groupby-aggregation.
 386: 
 387:     This class allows users to define their own custom aggregation in terms of
 388:     operations on Pandas dataframes in a map-reduce style. You need to specify
 389:     what operation to do on each chunk of data, how to combine those chunks of
 390:     data together, and then how to finalize the result.
 391: 
 392:     See :ref:`dataframe.groupby.aggregate` for more.
 393: 
 394:     Parameters
 395:     ----------
 396:     name : str
 397:         the name of the aggregation. It should be unique, since intermediate
 398:         result will be identified by this name.
 399:     chunk : callable
 400:         a function that will be called with the grouped column of each
 401:         partition. It can either return a single series or a tuple of series.
 402:         The index has to be equal to the groups.
 403:     agg : callable
 404:         a function that will be called to aggregate the results of each chunk.
 405:         Again the argument(s) will be grouped series. If ``chunk`` returned a
 406:         tuple, ``agg`` will be called with all of them as individual positional
 407:         arguments.
 408:     finalize : callable
 409:         an optional finalizer that will be called with the results from the
 410:         aggregation.
 411: 
 412:     Examples
 413:     --------
 414:     We could implement ``sum`` as follows:
 415: 
 416:     >>> custom_sum = dd.Aggregation(
 417:     ...     name='custom_sum',
 418:     ...     chunk=lambda s: s.sum(),
 419:     ...     agg=lambda s0: s0.sum()
 420:     ... )  # doctest: +SKIP
 421:     >>> df.groupby('g').agg(custom_sum)  # doctest: +SKIP
 422: 
 423:     We can implement ``mean`` as follows:
 424: 
 425:     >>> custom_mean = dd.Aggregation(
 426:     ...     name='custom_mean',
 427:     ...     chunk=lambda s: (s.count(), s.sum()),
 428:     ...     agg=lambda count, sum: (count.sum(), sum.sum()),
 429:     ...     finalize=lambda count, sum: sum / count,
 430:     ... )  # doctest: +SKIP
 431:     >>> df.groupby('g').agg(custom_mean)  # doctest: +SKIP
 432: 
 433:     Though of course, both of these are built-in and so you don't need to
 434:     implement them yourself.
 435:     """
 436: 
 437:     def __init__(self, name, chunk, agg, finalize=None):
 438:         self.chunk = chunk
 439:         self.agg = agg
 440:         self.finalize = finalize
 441:         self.__name__ = name
 442: 
 443: 
 444: def _groupby_aggregate(
 445:     df, aggfunc=None, levels=None, dropna=None, sort=False, observed=None, **kwargs
 446: ):
 447:     dropna = {"dropna": dropna} if dropna is not None else {}
 448:     observed = {"observed": observed} if observed is not None else {}
 449: 
 450:     with check_observed_deprecation():
 451:         grouped = df.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort, **observed, **dropna)
 452: 
 453:     # we emit a warning earlier in stack about default numeric_only being deprecated,
 454:     # so there's no need to propagate the warning that pandas emits as well
 455:     with check_numeric_only_deprecation():
 456:         return aggfunc(grouped, **kwargs)
 457: 
 458: 
 459: def _groupby_aggregate_spec(
 460:     df, spec, levels=None, dropna=None, sort=False, observed=None, **kwargs
 461: ):
 462:     """
 463:     A simpler version of _groupby_aggregate that just calls ``aggregate`` using
 464:     the user-provided spec.
 465:     """
 466:     dropna = {"dropna": dropna} if dropna is not None else {}
 467:     observed = {"observed": observed} if observed is not None else {}
 468:     return df.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort, **observed, **dropna).aggregate(
 469:         spec, **kwargs
 470:     )
 471: 
 472: 
 473: def _non_agg_chunk(df, *by, key, dropna=None, observed=None, **kwargs):
 474:     """
 475:     A non-aggregation agg function. This simuates the behavior of an initial
 476:     partitionwise aggregation, but doesn't actually aggregate or throw away
 477:     any data.
 478:     """
 479:     if is_series_like(df):
 480:         # Handle a series-like groupby. `by` could be columns that are not the series,
 481:         # but are like-indexed, so we handle that case by temporarily converting to
 482:         # a dataframe, then setting the index.
 483:         result = df.to_frame().set_index(by[0] if len(by) == 1 else list(by))[df.name]
 484:     else:
 485:         # Handle a frame-like groupby.
 486:         result = df.set_index(list(by))
 487:         if isinstance(key, (tuple, list, set, pd.Index)):
 488:             key = list(key)
 489:         result = result[key]
 490: 
 491:     # If observed is False, we have to check for categorical indices and possibly enrich
 492:     # them with unobserved values. This function is intended as an initial partitionwise
 493:     # aggregation, so you might expect that we could enrich the frame with unobserved
 494:     # values at the end. However, if you have multiple output partitions, that results
 495:     # in duplicated unobserved values in each partition. So we have to do this step
 496:     # at the start before any shuffling occurs so that we can consolidate all of the
 497:     # unobserved values in a single partition.
 498:     if observed is False:
 499:         has_categoricals = False
 500: 
 501:         # Search for categorical indices and get new index objects that have all the
 502:         # categories in them.
 503:         if isinstance(result.index, pd.CategoricalIndex):
 504:             has_categoricals = True
 505:             full_index = result.index.categories.copy().rename(result.index.name)
 506:         elif isinstance(result.index, pd.MultiIndex) and any(
 507:             isinstance(level, pd.CategoricalIndex) for level in result.index.levels
 508:         ):
 509:             has_categoricals = True
 510:             full_index = pd.MultiIndex.from_product(
 511:                 (
 512:                     level.categories
 513:                     if isinstance(level, pd.CategoricalIndex)
 514:                     else level
 515:                     for level in result.index.levels
 516:                 ),
 517:                 names=result.index.names,
 518:             )
 519:         if has_categoricals:
 520:             # If we found any categoricals, append unobserved values to the end of the
 521:             # frame.
 522:             new_cats = full_index[~full_index.isin(result.index)]
 523:             empty = pd.DataFrame(pd.NA, index=new_cats, columns=result.columns)
 524:             result = pd.concat([result, empty])
 525: 
 526:     return result
 527: 
 528: 
 529: def _apply_chunk(df, *by, dropna=None, observed=None, **kwargs):
 530:     func = kwargs.pop("chunk")
 531:     columns = kwargs.pop("columns")
 532:     dropna = {"dropna": dropna} if dropna is not None else {}
 533:     observed = {"observed": observed} if observed is not None else {}
 534: 
 535:     g = _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, by=by, **observed, **dropna)
 536:     if is_series_like(df) or columns is None:
 537:         return func(g, **kwargs)
 538:     else:
 539:         if isinstance(columns, (tuple, list, set, pd.Index)):
 540:             columns = list(columns)
 541:         return func(g[columns], **kwargs)
 542: 
 543: 
 544: def _var_chunk(df, *by):
 545:     if is_series_like(df):
 546:         df = df.to_frame()
 547: 
 548:     df = df.copy()
 549: 
 550:     g = _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, by=by)
 551:     with check_numeric_only_deprecation():
 552:         x = g.sum()
 553: 
 554:     n = g[x.columns].count().rename(columns=lambda c: (c, "-count"))
 555: 
 556:     cols = x.columns
 557:     df[cols] = df[cols] ** 2
 558: 
 559:     g2 = _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, by=by)
 560:     with check_numeric_only_deprecation():
 561:         x2 = g2.sum().rename(columns=lambda c: (c, "-x2"))
 562: 
 563:     return concat([x, x2, n], axis=1)
 564: 
 565: 
 566: def _var_combine(g, levels, sort=False):
 567:     return g.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort).sum()
 568: 
 569: 
 570: def _var_agg(g, levels, ddof, sort=False):
 571:     g = g.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort).sum()
 572:     nc = len(g.columns)
 573:     x = g[g.columns[: nc // 3]]
 574:     # chunks columns are tuples (value, name), so we just keep the value part
 575:     x2 = g[g.columns[nc // 3 : 2 * nc // 3]].rename(columns=lambda c: c[0])
 576:     n = g[g.columns[-nc // 3 :]].rename(columns=lambda c: c[0])
 577: 
 578:     # TODO: replace with _finalize_var?
 579:     result = x2 - x**2 / n
 580:     div = n - ddof
 581:     div[div < 0] = 0
 582:     result /= div
 583:     result[(n - ddof) == 0] = np.nan
 584:     assert is_dataframe_like(result)
 585:     result[result < 0] = 0  # avoid rounding errors that take us to zero
 586:     return result
 587: 
 588: 
 589: def _cov_combine(g, levels):
 590:     return g
 591: 
 592: 
 593: def _cov_finalizer(df, cols, std=False):
 594:     vals = []
 595:     num_elements = len(list(it.product(cols, repeat=2)))
 596:     num_cols = len(cols)
 597:     vals = list(range(num_elements))
 598:     col_idx_mapping = dict(zip(cols, range(num_cols)))
 599:     for i, j in it.combinations_with_replacement(df[cols].columns, 2):
 600:         x = col_idx_mapping[i]
 601:         y = col_idx_mapping[j]
 602:         idx = x + num_cols * y
 603:         mul_col = f"{i}{j}"
 604:         ni = df["%s-count" % i]
 605:         nj = df["%s-count" % j]
 606: 
 607:         n = np.sqrt(ni * nj)
 608:         div = n - 1
 609:         div[div < 0] = 0
 610:         val = (df[mul_col] - df[i] * df[j] / n).values[0] / div.values[0]
 611:         if std:
 612:             ii = f"{i}{i}"
 613:             jj = f"{j}{j}"
 614:             std_val_i = (df[ii] - (df[i] ** 2) / ni).values[0] / div.values[0]
 615:             std_val_j = (df[jj] - (df[j] ** 2) / nj).values[0] / div.values[0]
 616:             val = val / np.sqrt(std_val_i * std_val_j)
 617: 
 618:         vals[idx] = val
 619:         if i != j:
 620:             idx = num_cols * x + y
 621:             vals[idx] = val
 622: 
 623:     level_1 = cols
 624:     index = pd.MultiIndex.from_product([level_1, level_1])
 625:     return pd.Series(vals, index=index)
 626: 
 627: 
 628: def _mul_cols(df, cols):
 629:     """Internal function to be used with apply to multiply
 630:     each column in a dataframe by every other column
 631: 
 632:     a b c -> a*a, a*b, b*b, b*c, c*c
 633:     """
 634:     _df = df.__class__()
 635:     for i, j in it.combinations_with_replacement(cols, 2):
 636:         col = f"{i}{j}"
 637:         _df[col] = df[i] * df[j]
 638: 
 639:     # Fix index in a groupby().apply() context
 640:     # https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/8137
 641:     # https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/43568
 642:     # Make sure index dtype is int (even if _df is empty)
 643:     # https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9701
 644:     _df.index = np.zeros(len(_df), dtype=int)
 645:     return _df
 646: 
 647: 
 648: def _cov_chunk(df, *by):
 649:     """Covariance Chunk Logic
 650: 
 651:     Parameters
 652:     ----------
 653:     df : Pandas.DataFrame
 654:     std : bool, optional
 655:         When std=True we are calculating with Correlation
 656: 
 657:     Returns
 658:     -------
 659:     tuple
 660:         Processed X, Multiplied Cols,
 661:     """
 662:     if is_series_like(df):
 663:         df = df.to_frame()
 664:     df = df.copy()
 665: 
 666:     # mapping columns to str(numerical) values allows us to easily handle
 667:     # arbitrary column names (numbers, string, empty strings)
 668:     col_mapping = collections.OrderedDict()
 669:     for i, c in enumerate(df.columns):
 670:         col_mapping[c] = str(i)
 671:     df = df.rename(columns=col_mapping)
 672:     cols = df._get_numeric_data().columns
 673: 
 674:     # when grouping by external series don't exclude columns
 675:     is_mask = any(is_series_like(s) for s in by)
 676:     if not is_mask:
 677:         by = [col_mapping[k] for k in by]
 678:         cols = cols.drop(np.array(by))
 679: 
 680:     g = _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, by=by)
 681:     x = g.sum()
 682: 
 683:     mul = g.apply(_mul_cols, cols=cols).reset_index(level=-1, drop=True)
 684: 
 685:     n = g[x.columns].count().rename(columns=lambda c: f"{c}-count")
 686:     return (x, mul, n, col_mapping)
 687: 
 688: 
 689: def _cov_agg(_t, levels, ddof, std=False, sort=False):
 690:     sums = []
 691:     muls = []
 692:     counts = []
 693: 
 694:     # sometime we get a series back from concat combiner
 695:     t = list(_t)
 696: 
 697:     cols = t[0][0].columns
 698:     for x, mul, n, col_mapping in t:
 699:         sums.append(x)
 700:         muls.append(mul)
 701:         counts.append(n)
 702:         col_mapping = col_mapping
 703: 
 704:     total_sums = concat(sums).groupby(level=levels, sort=sort).sum()
 705:     total_muls = concat(muls).groupby(level=levels, sort=sort).sum()
 706:     total_counts = concat(counts).groupby(level=levels).sum()
 707:     result = (
 708:         concat([total_sums, total_muls, total_counts], axis=1)
 709:         .groupby(level=levels)
 710:         .apply(_cov_finalizer, cols=cols, std=std)
 711:     )
 712: 
 713:     inv_col_mapping = {v: k for k, v in col_mapping.items()}
 714:     idx_vals = result.index.names
 715:     idx_mapping = list()
 716: 
 717:     # when index is None we probably have selected a particular column
 718:     # df.groupby('a')[['b']].cov()
 719:     if len(idx_vals) == 1 and all(n is None for n in idx_vals):
 720:         idx_vals = list(inv_col_mapping.keys() - set(total_sums.columns))
 721: 
 722:     for val in idx_vals:
 723:         idx_name = inv_col_mapping.get(val, val)
 724:         idx_mapping.append(idx_name)
 725: 
 726:         if len(result.columns.levels[0]) < len(col_mapping):
 727:             # removing index from col_mapping (produces incorrect multiindexes)
 728:             try:
 729:                 col_mapping.pop(idx_name)
 730:             except KeyError:
 731:                 # when slicing the col_map will not have the index
 732:                 pass
 733: 
 734:     keys = list(col_mapping.keys())
 735:     for level in range(len(result.columns.levels)):
 736:         result.columns = result.columns.set_levels(keys, level=level)
 737: 
 738:     result.index.set_names(idx_mapping, inplace=True)
 739: 
 740:     # stacking can lead to a sorted index
 741:     s_result = result.stack(dropna=False)
 742:     assert is_dataframe_like(s_result)
 743:     return s_result
 744: 
 745: 
 746: ###############################################################
 747: # nunique
 748: ###############################################################
 749: def _drop_duplicates_reindex(df):
 750:     # Fix index in a groupby().apply() context
 751:     # https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/8137
 752:     # https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/43568
 753:     # Make sure index dtype is int (even if result is empty)
 754:     # https://github.com/dask/dask/pull/9701
 755:     result = df.drop_duplicates()
 756:     result.index = np.zeros(len(result), dtype=int)
 757:     return result
 758: 
 759: 
 760: def _nunique_df_chunk(df, *by, **kwargs):
 761:     name = kwargs.pop("name")
 762: 
 763:     g = _groupby_raise_unaligned(df, by=by)
 764:     if len(df) > 0:
 765:         grouped = (
 766:             g[[name]].apply(_drop_duplicates_reindex).reset_index(level=-1, drop=True)
 767:         )
 768:     else:
 769:         # Manually create empty version, since groupby-apply for empty frame
 770:         # results in df with no columns
 771:         grouped = g[[name]].nunique()
 772:         grouped = grouped.astype(df.dtypes[grouped.columns].to_dict())
 773: 
 774:     return grouped
 775: 
 776: 
 777: def _nunique_df_combine(df, levels, sort=False):
 778:     result = (
 779:         df.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort)
 780:         .apply(_drop_duplicates_reindex)
 781:         .reset_index(level=-1, drop=True)
 782:     )
 783:     return result
 784: 
 785: 
 786: def _nunique_df_aggregate(df, levels, name, sort=False):
 787:     with check_observed_deprecation():
 788:         return df.groupby(level=levels, sort=sort)[name].nunique()
 789: 
 790: 
 791: def _nunique_series_chunk(df, *by, **_ignored_):
 792:     # convert series to data frame, then hand over to dataframe code path
 793:     assert is_series_like(df)
 794: 
 795:     df = df.to_frame()
 796:     kwargs = dict(name=df.columns[0], levels=_determine_levels(by))
 797:     return _nunique_df_chunk(df, *by, **kwargs)
 798: 
 799: 
 800: ###############################################################
 801: # Aggregate support
 802: #
 803: # Aggregate is implemented as:
 804: #
 805: # 1. group-by-aggregate all partitions into intermediate values
 806: # 2. collect all partitions into a single partition
 807: # 3. group-by-aggregate the result into intermediate values
 808: # 4. transform all intermediate values into the result
 809: #
 810: # In Step 1 and 3 the dataframe is grouped on the same columns.
 811: #
 812: ###############################################################
 813: def _make_agg_id(func, column):
 814:     return f"{func!s}-{column!s}-{tokenize(func, column)}"
 815: 
 816: 
 817: def _normalize_spec(spec, non_group_columns):
 818:     """
 819:     Return a list of ``(result_column, func, input_column)`` tuples.
 820: 
 821:     Spec can be
 822: 
 823:     - a function
 824:     - a list of functions
 825:     - a dictionary that maps input-columns to functions
 826:     - a dictionary that maps input-columns to a lists of functions
 827:     - a dictionary that maps input-columns to a dictionaries that map
 828:       output-columns to functions.
 829: 
 830:     The non-group columns are a list of all column names that are not used in
 831:     the groupby operation.
 832: 
 833:     Usually, the result columns are mutli-level names, returned as tuples.
 834:     If only a single function is supplied or dictionary mapping columns
 835:     to single functions, simple names are returned as strings (see the first
 836:     two examples below).
 837: 
 838:     Examples
 839:     --------
 840:     >>> _normalize_spec('mean', ['a', 'b', 'c'])
 841:     [('a', 'mean', 'a'), ('b', 'mean', 'b'), ('c', 'mean', 'c')]
 842: 
 843:     >>> spec = collections.OrderedDict([('a', 'mean'), ('b', 'count')])
 844:     >>> _normalize_spec(spec, ['a', 'b', 'c'])
 845:     [('a', 'mean', 'a'), ('b', 'count', 'b')]
 846: 
 847:     >>> _normalize_spec(['var', 'mean'], ['a', 'b', 'c'])
 848:     ... # doctest: +NORMALIZE_WHITESPACE
 849:     [(('a', 'var'), 'var', 'a'), (('a', 'mean'), 'mean', 'a'), \
 850:      (('b', 'var'), 'var', 'b'), (('b', 'mean'), 'mean', 'b'), \
 851:      (('c', 'var'), 'var', 'c'), (('c', 'mean'), 'mean', 'c')]
 852: 
 853:     >>> spec = collections.OrderedDict([('a', 'mean'), ('b', ['sum', 'count'])])
 854:     >>> _normalize_spec(spec, ['a', 'b', 'c'])
 855:     ... # doctest: +NORMALIZE_WHITESPACE
 856:     [(('a', 'mean'), 'mean', 'a'), (('b', 'sum'), 'sum', 'b'), \
 857:       (('b', 'count'), 'count', 'b')]
 858: 
 859:     >>> spec = collections.OrderedDict()
 860:     >>> spec['a'] = ['mean', 'size']
 861:     >>> spec['b'] = collections.OrderedDict([('e', 'count'), ('f', 'var')])
 862:     >>> _normalize_spec(spec, ['a', 'b', 'c'])
 863:     ... # doctest: +NORMALIZE_WHITESPACE
 864:     [(('a', 'mean'), 'mean', 'a'), (('a', 'size'), 'size', 'a'), \
 865:      (('b', 'e'), 'count', 'b'), (('b', 'f'), 'var', 'b')]
 866:     """
 867:     if not isinstance(spec, dict):
 868:         spec = collections.OrderedDict(zip(non_group_columns, it.repeat(spec)))
 869: 
 870:     res = []
 871: 
 872:     if isinstance(spec, dict):
 873:         for input_column, subspec in spec.items():
 874:             if isinstance(subspec, dict):
 875:                 res.extend(
 876:                     ((input_column, result_column), func, input_column)
 877:                     for result_column, func in subspec.items()
 878:                 )
 879: 
 880:             else:
 881:                 if not isinstance(subspec, list):
 882:                     subspec = [subspec]
 883: 
 884:                 res.extend(
 885:                     ((input_column, funcname(func)), func, input_column)
 886:                     for func in subspec
 887:                 )
 888: 
 889:     else:
 890:         raise ValueError(f"unsupported agg spec of type {type(spec)}")
 891: 
 892:     compounds = (list, tuple, dict)
 893:     use_flat_columns = not any(
 894:         isinstance(subspec, compounds) for subspec in spec.values()
 895:     )
 896: 
 897:     if use_flat_columns:
 898:         res = [(input_col, func, input_col) for (_, func, input_col) in res]
 899: 
 900:     return res
 901: 
 902: 
 903: def _build_agg_args(spec):
 904:     """
 905:     Create transformation functions for a normalized aggregate spec.
 906: 
 907:     Parameters
 908:     ----------
 909:     spec: a list of (result-column, aggregation-function, input-column) triples.
 910:         To work with all argument forms understood by pandas use
 911:         ``_normalize_spec`` to normalize the argment before passing it on to
 912:         ``_build_agg_args``.
 913: 
 914:     Returns
 915:     -------
 916:     chunk_funcs: a list of (intermediate-column, function, keyword) triples
 917:         that are applied on grouped chunks of the initial dataframe.
 918: 
 919:     agg_funcs: a list of (intermediate-column, functions, keyword) triples that
 920:         are applied on the grouped concatination of the preprocessed chunks.
 921: 
 922:     finalizers: a list of (result-column, function, keyword) triples that are
 923:         applied after the ``agg_funcs``. They are used to create final results
 924:         from intermediate representations.
 925:     """
 926:     known_np_funcs = {
 927:         np.min: "min",
 928:         np.max: "max",
 929:         np.median: "median",
 930:         np.std: "std",
 931:         np.var: "var",
 932:     }
 933: 
 934:     # check that there are no name conflicts for a single input column
 935:     by_name = {}
 936:     for _, func, input_column in spec:
 937:         key = funcname(known_np_funcs.get(func, func)), input_column
 938:         by_name.setdefault(key, []).append((func, input_column))
 939: 
 940:     for funcs in by_name.values():
 941:         if len(funcs) != 1:
 942:             raise ValueError(f"conflicting aggregation functions: {funcs}")
 943: 
 944:     chunks = {}
 945:     aggs = {}
 946:     finalizers = []
 947: 
 948:     # a partial may contain some arguments, pass them down
 949:     # https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/9615
 950:     for result_column, func, input_column in spec:
 951:         func_args = ()
 952:         func_kwargs = {}
 953:         if isinstance(func, partial):
 954:             func_args, func_kwargs = func.args, func.keywords
 955: 
 956:         if not isinstance(func, Aggregation):
 957:             func = funcname(known_np_funcs.get(func, func))
 958: 
 959:         impls = _build_agg_args_single(
 960:             result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column
 961:         )
 962: 
 963:         # overwrite existing result-columns, generate intermediates only once
 964:         for spec in impls["chunk_funcs"]:
 965:             chunks[spec[0]] = spec
 966:         for spec in impls["aggregate_funcs"]:
 967:             aggs[spec[0]] = spec
 968: 
 969:         finalizers.append(impls["finalizer"])
 970: 
 971:     chunks = sorted(chunks.values())
 972:     aggs = sorted(aggs.values())
 973: 
 974:     return chunks, aggs, finalizers
 975: 
 976: 
 977: def _build_agg_args_single(result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column):
 978:     simple_impl = {
 979:         "sum": (M.sum, M.sum),
 980:         "min": (M.min, M.min),
 981:         "max": (M.max, M.max),
 982:         "count": (M.count, M.sum),
 983:         "size": (M.size, M.sum),
 984:         "first": (M.first, M.first),
 985:         "last": (M.last, M.last),
 986:         "prod": (M.prod, M.prod),
 987:         "median": (
 988:             None,
 989:             M.median,
 990:         ),  # No chunk func for median, we can only take it when aggregating
 991:     }
 992: 
 993:     if func in simple_impl.keys():
 994:         return _build_agg_args_simple(
 995:             result_column, func, input_column, simple_impl[func]
 996:         )
 997: 
 998:     elif func == "var":
 999:         return _build_agg_args_var(
1000:             result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column
1001:         )
1002: 
1003:     elif func == "std":
1004:         return _build_agg_args_std(
1005:             result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column
1006:         )
1007: 
1008:     elif func == "mean":
1009:         return _build_agg_args_mean(result_column, func, input_column)
1010: 
1011:     elif func == "list":
1012:         return _build_agg_args_list(result_column, func, input_column)
1013: 
1014:     elif isinstance(func, Aggregation):
1015:         return _build_agg_args_custom(result_column, func, input_column)
1016: 
1017:     else:
1018:         raise ValueError(f"unknown aggregate {func}")
1019: 
1020: 
1021: def _build_agg_args_simple(result_column, func, input_column, impl_pair):
1022:     intermediate = _make_agg_id(func, input_column)
1023:     chunk_impl, agg_impl = impl_pair
1024: 
1025:     return dict(
1026:         chunk_funcs=[
1027:             (
1028:                 intermediate,
1029:                 _apply_func_to_column,
1030:                 dict(column=input_column, func=chunk_impl),
1031:             )
1032:         ],
1033:         aggregate_funcs=[
1034:             (
1035:                 intermediate,
1036:                 _apply_func_to_column,
1037:                 dict(column=intermediate, func=agg_impl),
1038:             )
1039:         ],
1040:         finalizer=(result_column, itemgetter(intermediate), dict()),
1041:     )
1042: 
1043: 
1044: def _build_agg_args_var(result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column):
1045:     int_sum = _make_agg_id("sum", input_column)
1046:     int_sum2 = _make_agg_id("sum2", input_column)
1047:     int_count = _make_agg_id("count", input_column)
1048: 
1049:     # we don't expect positional args here
1050:     if func_args:
1051:         raise TypeError(
1052:             f"aggregate function '{func}' doesn't support positional arguments, but got {func_args}"
1053:         )
1054: 
1055:     # and we only expect ddof=N in kwargs
1056:     expected_kwargs = {"ddof"}
1057:     unexpected_kwargs = func_kwargs.keys() - expected_kwargs
1058:     if unexpected_kwargs:
1059:         raise TypeError(
1060:             f"aggregate function '{func}' supports {expected_kwargs} keyword arguments, but got {unexpected_kwargs}"
1061:         )
1062: 
1063:     return dict(
1064:         chunk_funcs=[
1065:             (int_sum, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=input_column, func=M.sum)),
1066:             (int_count, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=input_column, func=M.count)),
1067:             (int_sum2, _compute_sum_of_squares, dict(column=input_column)),
1068:         ],
1069:         aggregate_funcs=[
1070:             (col, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=col, func=M.sum))
1071:             for col in (int_sum, int_count, int_sum2)
1072:         ],
1073:         finalizer=(
1074:             result_column,
1075:             _finalize_var,
1076:             dict(
1077:                 sum_column=int_sum,
1078:                 count_column=int_count,
1079:                 sum2_column=int_sum2,
1080:                 **func_kwargs,
1081:             ),
1082:         ),
1083:     )
1084: 
1085: 
1086: def _build_agg_args_std(result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column):
1087:     impls = _build_agg_args_var(
1088:         result_column, func, func_args, func_kwargs, input_column
1089:     )
1090: 
1091:     result_column, _, kwargs = impls["finalizer"]
1092:     impls["finalizer"] = (result_column, _finalize_std, kwargs)
1093: 
1094:     return impls
1095: 
1096: 
1097: def _build_agg_args_mean(result_column, func, input_column):
1098:     int_sum = _make_agg_id("sum", input_column)
1099:     int_count = _make_agg_id("count", input_column)
1100: 
1101:     return dict(
1102:         chunk_funcs=[
1103:             (int_sum, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=input_column, func=M.sum)),
1104:             (int_count, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=input_column, func=M.count)),
1105:         ],
1106:         aggregate_funcs=[
1107:             (col, _apply_func_to_column, dict(column=col, func=M.sum))
1108:             for col in (int_sum, int_count)
1109:         ],
1110:         finalizer=(
1111:             result_column,
1112:             _finalize_mean,
1113:             dict(sum_column=int_sum, count_column=int_count),
1114:         ),
1115:     )
1116: 
1117: 
1118: def _build_agg_args_list(result_column, func, input_column):
1119:     intermediate = _make_agg_id("list", input_column)
1120: 
1121:     return dict(
1122:         chunk_funcs=[
1123:             (
1124:                 intermediate,
1125:                 _apply_func_to_column,
1126:                 dict(column=input_column, func=lambda s: s.apply(list)),
1127:             )
1128:         ],
1129:         aggregate_funcs=[
1130:             (
1131:                 intermediate,
1132:                 _apply_func_to_column,
1133:                 dict(
1134:                     column=intermediate,
1135:                     func=lambda s0: s0.apply(
1136:                         lambda chunks: list(it.chain.from_iterable(chunks))
1137:                     ),
1138:                 ),
1139:             )
1140:         ],
1141:         finalizer=(result_column, itemgetter(intermediate), dict()),
1142:     )
1143: 
1144: 
1145: def _build_agg_args_custom(result_column, func, input_column):
1146:     col = _make_agg_id(funcname(func), input_column)
1147: 
1148:     if func.finalize is None:
1149:         finalizer = (result_column, operator.itemgetter(col), dict())
1150: 
1151:     else:
1152:         finalizer = (
1153:             result_column,
1154:             _apply_func_to_columns,
1155:             dict(func=func.finalize, prefix=col),
1156:         )
1157: 
1158:     return dict(
1159:         chunk_funcs=[
1160:             (col, _apply_func_to_column, dict(func=func.chunk, column=input_column))
1161:         ],
1162:         aggregate_funcs=[
1163:             (col, _apply_func_to_columns, dict(func=func.agg, prefix=col))
1164:         ],
1165:         finalizer=finalizer,
1166:     )
